---
title: "ECNS 560 Project Report"
author: "Cooper Craig and Nicholas Starring"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: lumen
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,      # Hide code
  error = FALSE,     # Hide error messages
  warning = FALSE,   # Hide warnings (like "NAs introduced")
  message = FALSE,   # Hide package loading messages
  fig.align = "center" # Centers all your plots automatically
)
```

# Project Introduction, Topic, and Motivation

### Topic and Motivation

For this project, we aim to better understand recessions and develop a potential indicator. A recession is usually defined as two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth. More generally, recessions (or similar events) may be thought of as when the economy goes beyond "slowing down" and begins shrinking in some aspect. This is, or should be, of interest to almost every economic agent, from policymakers and business leaders to households and individuals. A recession can have many impacts, but may generally be classified into two categories: either a "financial crisis" or a "labor recession." It is clear that a financial crisis is of interest to everyone with assets---if the value of assets is decreasing, then it is reasonable to expect that there will be massive income effects as these agents "feel poorer" and respond rationally. If there is a labor recession, households are likely to struggle to provide enough labor to satisfy their needs, and policymakers would like to mitigate this.

Existing predictors of recessions are commonly flawed and overfit---there is a saying that "economists have predicted 11 of the last 5 recessions." This stems from identifying some condition which has occurred prior to most or all of the recessions observed in the United States based on a single (or a relatively small number) indicator variable. Perhaps the most famous of these is the inversion of the yield curve on US treasury notes. While this may occur prior to most recessions, it is a sentiment-based indicator rather than a structural indicator. Further, it is imprecise, commonly referenced as indicating a recession sometime in the next 6--12 months. This presents obvious issues, as regular false positives and an uncertain timeline prevent preparatory or preventative actions from being reasonably taken.

The metric we propose reduces this dependence on so little information. It is not particularly obvious how a good predictor might be constructed; one of the most significant challenges is the lack of data, as recessions occur somewhat infrequently. This makes any attempt at prediction subject to overfitting, though we aim to construct robustness via out-of-sample testing---a necessary characteristic of the machine learning methods we use.

### Principal Methodology: PH and Wasserstein Distance

We attempt to understand whether a recession can be signaled by a change in the "velocity" of the economy, and we define this velocity as the Wasserstein distance between two consecutive states. To achieve this, we use two techniques not yet assimilated into mainstream empirical economics: Persistent Homology (PH) and the Wasserstein distance.

#### Persistent Homology (PH)

PH is a tool which models the "shape" of data---how discrete data points are embedded in a high-dimensional manifold in space---and allows for unsupervised clustering and structuring. In detail, PH studies emergent structures in the data and at what observational scale these structures exist. It does this by setting a ball of radius $r$ around each point in space, then varying the size of $r$. When these balls overlap, an edge is added between them. This forms a graph, where the data points are the vertices and the edges are a function of the size of the radius. If PH is taken further, there are additional degrees where it may be extended: if three points are fully connected, then they enclose a triangular "face", and if four points are fully connected, then they enclose a tetrahedronal volume. This space-filling volume may be taken to whatever extent desired, which is the study of simplicial complexes. For the purpose of this study, we stop at the two-dimensional faces, so consider only the edges and faces.

#### Wasserstein Distance

The Wasserstein distance is a measure of optimal transport between two structures. It is commonly known as the "earth-mover's distance," as it measures the distance between two probability distributions via minimal shifting of this probability---hence the comparison to moving earth, where this distance is the amount of dirt which must be moved to make pile number (1) equivalent in shape to pile number (2). In a physics sense, this is a measure of work, and in a more purely mathematical sense, it functions as a topological derivative---the rate of change between topological states.

We create a time series of this distance as we move a window of length $k$ along the data, creating PH structures for windows containing time steps 1:k, 2:(k+1), ..., n:(n+k-1). The overlap in these windows means that measuring the distance between consecutive structures is a measure of the difference in influence of the oldest (exiting) point and the newest (entering) point. If these points are identical, the PH structures are identical and the distance is zero. If they are strongly influential in different aspects, this distance is very large. It is our hypothesis that this distance will act as an edge detector between states, and in particular, the entry or exit from a recessionary economic state.

### Evaluation Metrics: ROC and AUC

Given the above methodology, we use Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis and Area Under the Curve (AUC) as evaluation metrics.

ROC compares the false positives and true positives of a binary classifier. For this metric, some threshold $\tau$ is chosen for the Wasserstein metric; if the metric is greater than $\tau$, then a recession is signaled. This charts the sensitivity (true positive rate) on the Y-axis and the complement of the specificity (false positive rate) on the X-axis. This curve will necessarily form an arc between (0,0), where the model detects no recessions (due to a threshold set too high), and (1,1), where the model signals everything as a recession (due to a threshold set too low). Assuming a symmetric cost of misclassification, the optimal threshold value $\tau^*$ is chosen as the $\tau$ corresponding to the point on this curve closest to (0,1), for perfect detection of true positives without any false indications.

The AUC is the area under the ROC. It measures the overall quality of the signal from this model on a scale from 0 to 1; we can interpret this as the probability that this model will assign a higher Wasserstein distance to a recessionary window than to a non-recessionary window. This should be within the interval (0.5, 1), because if it is worse than random, we would simply invert the signal. This metric is less sensitive to the potential to "get lucky" with careful selection of the threshold $\tau$ than many alternatives, because it considers all thresholds and the power of the model over all variations of this parameter. Further, as a metric derived from ROC, the AUC is invariant on the partition of the data between classes, and so will not be artificially inflated by an imbalance between them in the training data.

## Data and Processing

Our data is retrieved from Yahoo Finance (via the "tidyquant" R package) and from FRED (also via the "tidyquant" R package). This data includes time series for the daily close of major stock indices (S&P 500, DOW, NASDAQ, Russel 2000), common hedges for uncertainty (gold, silver, Case-Schiller Index for real estate), and monthly data on macroeconomic data (U3 and U6 unemployment, GDP, CPI, Federal Funds Rate, Housing Starts, ...) (A full data appendix is found in the [*clean_data.R*]{.underline} script). Additionally, API sourcing is used to collect two measures of consumer sentiment around recessions; google search trend data for "recession" and wikipedia page visits for "recession", to better obtain a sort of "panic" measure that may be useful in predicting recessions. Collectively, these present a multi-faceted image of the economy at a point in time -- in particular, they represent both classical macroeconomic indicators and aggregating signals for the "shadow economy" of asset prices and volatility hedges. Further, the vast majority of our data begins well before 2000, with some stretching back to the beginning of the 20th century, a visualization attached below will demonstrate that. Recession data is manually collected into a dataframe for easy joining with the larger dataset, the data is from the NBER. Lagged indicators for if a recession starts in the next 6, 12 and 18 months are created from this data and will serve as our target predicted variable in terms of forecasting recessions.

The vast majority of our data is observed monthly, therefore, we collapse our more granular data (like asset prices) to a monthly level, creating a monthly mean close price and an interday volatility measure from the variance of these daily closing prices within the month. This collapsed data is combined with the other data, merging on the **primary key of year-month**, at which point **a single primary key** for our cleaned dataset is created.

Additionally, linear imputation was performed on the consumer sentiment variable, which was observed quarterly until the late 1970's, following this though, it too is observed on a monthly basis.

It is also worth noting again that there is a "data appendix" in the "clean_data.R" script containing much more detailed descriptions of each variable and its source.

From this, we do some further processing, to create a relative-scaled first-difference dataset. Importantly, this relative-scaled first-difference is computed as a direct first-difference for those values which are already in percentage terms (interest rates or unemployment), and similarly, we also use this direct first-differencing for mean-reverting series, to avoid seeing explosive growth around zero or negligible percent changes for large level-valued changes as it reaches peaks or troughs. Both the transformed and the untransformed data can be useful, but the first difference and relative scaling normalizes and whitens the data, reducing issues of autocorrelation and scale differences between variables. These transformations are also then formally normalized, as that is what persistent homology requires to work properly.

Finally, we append some functions of our variables onto that dataset to allow for a more robust approach when using persistent homology. In particular, we use log-returns for our asset data and other compounding indices, which tend to exhibit exponential growth and would thus distort the spacial "shape" of the data we intend to exploit in such a manner as to dominate any further processes, despite its failure as a dominant indicator of recessions.

In terms of missing data, the only missingness of concern is the lack of duration that some features have data for. Aside from that, missing values are of little concern at this stage, keeping in mind our temporal restrictions. For a better demonstration of the ranges of our variables of interest, observe the following chart.

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="110%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="All of the plots are included in the MacroVars_EDA.R script"}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/data_coverage_timeline.png")
```

To ensure the robustness and validity of the data, an analysis of outliers is also carried out on the data, using the *dlookr* package in R. Using this tool, we discovered that while there are extreme values, they are naturally occurring, as most can be explained by looking at the historical period in which they lay. This analysis also shed light to the variability of some of our variables, with certain ones being composed of \~35% outlier observations. With all that said, we will keep these extreme values, as not only are they "real observations", but also are vital for the shocks that we are looking for in our Persistent homology approach.

These steps were carried out in the direction of the "**data cleaning checklist**", which is applied completely in the "clean_data.R" script, though due to the formalized and clean nature of the data we bring in, the data is quite "clean" already.

### Analysis of Macroeconomic Data

Prior to processing our data into topological structures, to predict recessions it is important to know if we have ground to stand on in the first place. In that, we need to determine if these variables, and their relationships meaningfully differ when the economy is in a recession, or near one.

We do so by choosing two representative variables, that are often named as some of the most important macroeconomic indicators; the federal funds rate and the u3 unemployment rate. We restrict our exploratory data analysis to just these variables since doing every subset of the variables in our dataset would be impractical, and it is not unrealistic to think that the lessons learned from just these variables can be applied to many of the others in our data set owing to the theoretical interconnecitvity of all of our variables.

With each of these two variables, conditional distributions are created, where we look to see if the distribution of either variable is notably different depending on if the month of observation was recessionary or not. For both variables, whether looking at levels or measures of monthly change, the distributions are different in respect to their month's recessionary status, for example,

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="70%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="All of the plots are included in the MacroVars_EDA.R script"}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/fedfunds_level_by_regime.png")
```

These findings give us foundational reasoning to suspect that there are different, detectable regimes with these variables depending on if the economy is in a recession or not, giving credence to the idea that recessionary periods have different topological structures than stable periods do. This implyies that our Wasserstein metric prediction method may have some weight behind it. But again, this is very cursory evidence, and shouldn't be seen as definitive in any sort.

To gain more momentum behind our topological approach, it is also of use to see if the relationships between these two variables changes dependent on recessionary status, as this is ultimately what our topological approach hinges upon. We plot the two variables against eachother, both in levels and rates, to see if their relationship changes dependent on economic stability, and find visual evidence across both levels and rates that there is some change in the relationship, particularly when looking at if the period prior to a recession (as that is ultimately what we want to predict-if we're in a month with a recession upcoming). If there were no regime-effect, we'd see the red dots evenly distrbuted among the grey dots.

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="70%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="All of the plots are included in the MacroVars_EDA.R script"}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/scatter_fedfunds_u3_levels_lag12.png")
```

We also examine how the two variables vary together through time by looking at a joint time series between the two against shaded recession periods. These plots show a slight relationship, and but certainly nothing that is visibly noteworthy. These plots are interactive and are found in the MacroVars_EDA.R script. They are not included here to reduce cluttering, keeping in mind the rubric.

We also conduct an analysis of extreme values for these two variables and find notable outliers for both, but since both are true, factual data, which can be explained by events at the time, they are retained. Their retention is also necessary when considering our topological approach, as the Wasserstein distances will be driven by large shocks that an outlier causes. This extreme value analysis was then expanded to every variable, and as touched on in the first section of this report, no outliers will be excluded from analysis, as they are all "real" datum, and drive our Wasserstein TDA approach.

### Analysis of Topological Data

As is outlined in the introduction, our analysis will be examining the "distance" between topological structures from one time window to the next using Wasserstein distances (which are calculated for each dimension of topological structure in a given window). These distances will be the variables that will be used to predict if a month is near an upcoming recession or not. A more thorough description of our process is found in the methodology section.

With that said, due to unequal data coverage, computational limitations, and lessons from other similar empirical approaches, we don't and cannot use our full data in our TDA approach (Gidea & Katz, 2018; Ismail et al. 2022). Instead, we select multiple groupings of variables that combine both a good amount of data availability (the data availability for a group will only be as good as its "most missing" member variable), and theoretical relevance to recessions. These included groupings of "monetary movers" (unemployment, inflation, fed funds), "widespread panic" (Google recession trends, S&P500 volatility, U6 unemployment), "real economy" (consumer sentiment, industrial production, u3 unemployment, housing starts), and others, which are all analyzed equally in the WassDist_DEA.R script.

To gain a fundamental understanding of the structure of the relationships between the variables, a function wass created that plots either 2 or 3 variable overlapping temporal windows of 12 months as time progresses, with coloring to indicate if the economy is approaching a recession or is in one. For a 2d example, consider the following subsection of our "real economy" collection of variables discussed earlier.

::: {style="display: flex; align-items: center;"}
::: {style="width: 80%;"}
```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE,fig.cap="Press the play button or drag the slider to see the point cloud change over time"}
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

my_interactive_plot = readRDS("../output/interactive_plotly_plots/real_economy_2d.rds")

my_interactive_plot %>% 
  layout(
    xaxis = list(range = c(-3, 3)),
    yaxis = list(range = c(-3, 3)),
    margin = list(r = 0) # Kills the right margin so it sits flush with the key
  )
```
:::

::: {style="width: 40%; padding-left: 10px;"}
```{r, echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/key.jpg")
```
:::
:::

Most of these 2d plots had the same findings, in that it is difficult to say that structural divergence happens more frequently in the lead up to recessions. For some specifications, the structure of the data seemed to change leading to a recession, but it is difficult to know if that is just random divergence. Three dimensional plots were employed alongside the 2d plots, testing for the same structural divergence in anticipation of a recession. For an example, we can model all of our "monetary mover" variables over time.

::: {style="display: flex; align-items: center;"}
::: {style="width: 80%;"}
```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE,fig.cap="Press the play button or drag the slider to see the point cloud change over time"}
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

my_interactive_plot = readRDS("../output/interactive_plotly_plots/monetary_3d.rds")

my_interactive_plot
```
:::

::: {style="width: 40%; padding-left: 10px;"}
```{r, echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("../output/visualizations/key.jpg")
```
:::
:::

It is worth noting that the mesh covering the dots is not indicative of any sort of exact topological structure that will be uncovered when using persistent homology, and serves more as a visual aid.

Again, we don't find any sort of silver bullet here indicating that our approach may work. With that said, persistent homology may be better able to capture relationships within data, relationships that the human eye isn't keen to detect given a 3d graph, even with the mesh applied over the variables.

Following the point cloud maps, a different methodology is tried to get a foundational grasp as to the promise of the Wasserstein-distance TDA approach. Here, we calculate the wasserstein distance for a set window size, and plot it against recessions. Here is an example from the subset of variables that were observed prior to the great depression. Again, these are wasserstein distances, a measure of "how hard" it would be to reconstruct one topological structure from another (this is an imprecise definition). Here the window size is 18 months.

```{r, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.height=5, fig.cap="Narrow the window and drag the slider to see the change through time."}
library(dygraphs)
library(xts)
library(dplyr)

source("../scripts/PH_functions.R")

wass_data = read.csv("../data/clean/pre_depression_wasses.csv") %>%
  select(-X) %>%
  mutate(window2_end = as.Date(window2_end))

full_data = read.csv("../data/clean/transformed_cleaned_data.csv") %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(date))

plot_distances(
  dist_data = wass_data,
  full_data = full_data,
  dimensions = c("dim0"),
  subtitle = "0th dimension Wasserstein"
)
plot_distances(
  dist_data = wass_data,
  full_data = full_data,
  dimensions = c("dim1", "dim2"),
  subtitle = "1st and 2nd dimension Wasserstein"
)
```

For each of the groupings of variables, plots like this were assembled. The results from these plots are more uplifting than the point clouds, but still need to be approached with caution, for example, when looking at the bottom plot, showing the 1st dimension Wasserstein over time note how there are some spikes that occur quite close to recessions (1948, 1953, 1990, etc.) but it is difficult to argue that these aren't just well-placed noise-that is what our prediction methods will attempt to tease out.

With this in mind, after weighing the predictive power evident in this type of time series plot, in conjunction with data availability constraints, we choose 3 specifications to focus our predictive analysis on.

Namely, we choose the following (where all variables are differenced and scaled, as outlined in the *alter_data.R* script.

-   **The pre-depression variables**

    -   Gold price, Industrial Production, S&P 500 Average Close, S&P 500 Volatility

-   **The "real economy" variables**

    -   Consumer Sentiment, Industrial Production, U3 Unemployment, Housing Starts

-   **The "monetary movers" variables**

    -   U3 Unemployment, Inflation, Federal Funds Rate

# Results

As is outlined in the introduction, our analysis examines the distance between topological structures from one time window to the next using the Wasserstein metric (which are calculated for each dimension of topological structure in a given window). These distances will be the variables that will be used to predict if a month is near an upcoming recession or not. A more thorough description of our process is found in the methodology section.

With that said, due to unequal data coverage, computational limitations, and lessons from other similar empirical approaches, we do not and cannot use our full data in our TDA approach (Gidea & Katz, 2018; Ismail et al. 2022). Instead, we select multiple groupings of variables that combine both a good amount of data availability (the data availability for a group will only be as good as its "most missing" member variable), and theoretical relevance to recessions. These included groupings of "monetary movers" (yields on 1 year and 10 year treasuries and the federal funds rate), "pre-depression" (those variables for which the data reaches back at least to the era of the Great Depression), and "real economy" (those variables which measure characteristics of real assets, their values, and their construction).

```{r}
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
source("../scripts/prediction_functions.R") 

results_spec_pre_dep = read.csv("../data/clean/spec_pre_dep_results_raw.csv")
results_spec_real_econ = read.csv("../data/clean/spec_real_econ_results_raw.csv")
results_spec_monetary_movers = read.csv("../data/clean/spec_monetary_movers_results_raw.csv")
```

In particular, we use the zero-th, first, and second dimensional Wasserstein metrics, with their pairwise interactions, as predictor variables in a logit and in a lasso regression. These models are trained on the pre-2000 data, and AUC metrics are reported for in the test data after 2000.

The results for the monetary movers specification are below.

```{r}
format_results_table(results_spec_monetary_movers, "Monetary Movers Specification Performance")
```

The first column describes the goal prediction metric: whether there will be a recession in the next 6, 12, or 18 months. The second and third columns provide the specification for how the Wasserstein metric was computed -- whether the windows were overlapping, and how long the windows were. To clarify, if the windows overlap, then we may see consecutive windows as months 1-12 and 2-13, and if not, they might look like 1-12 and 13-24. Following this, we have the out-of-sample lasso and logit model AUC values, and some model specifications: the $\lambda$ value for the lasso regression and the features which were not compressed to zero in the lasso regression. These features indicate the Wasserstein metric values at zero dimensions (connectivity), one dimension (holes), and two dimensions (voids), and the interactions of these time series.

It is of particular note that very few of these specifications exceed 0.5 in the AUC computation. This, as touched on in the introduction, indicates that they perform worse-than-random on the out-of-sample data. There are a couple values which do exceed 0.5, the largest of which is 0.595 for an 18 month overlapping window, attempting to predict a recession 6 months out using a lasso regression. Extending this window length to 36 months also produces an AUC greater than 0.5, but at 0.565, less than that of the 18 month window.

Similar tables for the pre-depression and real economy specifications are below.

```{r}
format_results_table(results_spec_pre_dep, "Pre-Depression Specification Performance")
format_results_table(results_spec_real_econ, "Real Economy Specification Performance")
```

Much as in the monetary movers specification, there are several values in each of these below 0.5, so performing worse-than-random. However, there are far fewer of these, relative to the number of estimates, and there are several more extreme AUC values, approaching one. In particular, the 8 month non-overlapping windows generated an AUC of about 0.8 when predicting recessions 12 months out with the real economy specification for the model.

As a broad category, the real economy seems to be the most reliable, with (by inspection) the largest proportion of models with better-than-random performance in the test set. This seems reasonable, since a recession is defined based on GDP, which most closely fits in this "real economy" category, so the structure and manifold on which these parameters move is more relevant. This real economy specification also seems to have the least number of models substantially under 0.5, again supporting this hypothesis of it performing the best.

It is also worth noting which variables were most persistent in the models, that is, which variables were least commonly removed by the lasso regression. D0 and D1 were the most common of these (by inspection) followed by interactions with and between these as well as D2. This suggests that the second dimension, looking for voids is least important, while connectivity and holes are relatively more important. This, if we attempt to interpret it, likely suggests that a cyclical economy or two distinct behaviors of the economy is either (1) more important of an observation than persistant voids, or (2) we lacked sufficient data to identify persistant voids -- this could have either been longer histories (where we would then be lacking enough folds for robust cross validation) or data with a greater time-density.

# Conclusion

These results are potentially promising, demonstrating that there may be some value to Wasserstein metrics and persistent homology as a methodology.  Particularly for the real economic variable specification set, there were many models with (nominally) an AUC metric greater than 0.5, indicating better-than-random performance, with both the lasso model and with the logit model.  Further, the lasso model selected the D0 and D1 measure on a somewhat consistent basis, which may suggest that these hold more intrinsic meaning and relevance to the potential of recessions in the future.  This is not only relevant to the prediction of recessions, but also to better understand the movement and shape of the macro-economy.

Despite the potential of these results, they are limited by data.  As was mentioned in the introduction, recessions are relatively infrequent, so we have limited data and variations on structure or causation to fully generalize.  Further, this infrequency presents difficulty for robust training -- it does not make sense to have many folds for cross validation, when it is possible or even likely that many of those folds may have only one or even zero recessions for the model to learn from.  The remaining major limitation on data is that for both training and for the final testing of the model, we do not have enough data to reasonably throw out a large section to truly isolate our testing set; with the autocorrelation present in most time series, it is common practice to discard a segment of data between training and validation, and between training and testing, in order to avoid information contamination.  It is our judgement that given the data we do have, we gain enough statistical power to justify this likely bias as a tradeoff.  Further research would ideally address these issues, as well as retain enough validation data to test the best performing models from the tables presented in the Results section on never-before-seen data, to better justify whether they are a result of chance or a truly predictive model.

These results are also limited by the computational power available to us, as we are unable to reasonably construct confidence intervals for these AUC metrics.  In an ideal world, this may be done with the use of a variety of bootstrap and non-parametric methods; the two most likely candidates being resampling and restimating, possibly with varients to better address the time-series characteristics of the data, and to reshuffle the labels, to test if there are a comparable number of apparently significant models when there is known to be no structure.

Despite these failures in each particular model, as well as the potential risk of "p-hacking", it is the author's belief that the apparent improvement of the models based on the real economic specification over those based on monetary movers and pre-depression is a significant finding, as is the persistence of D0 (connected components) and D1 (holes) as selected predictors in the lasso regressions.  These go to support existing literature on the disconnect between the "real economy" of production, labor, and goods, and the "shadow economy" of volatile, high-frequency assets, and potentially motivate further study on how the shape and underlying structure of the macro-economy shifts through time, and what characteristics of that structure are most important to its behavior.
